narrative: As a Black male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 35, your academic profile also includes being in the lowest decile for both your first-year and third-year school grades, alongside an upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time. The university's admission model, trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try, estimated a 72.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on just a few key factors from your background that stood out as the strongest influences. Most critically, your third-year school grades, placing you in the bottom decile compared to the typical student's middle-decile performance, pushed the model heavily toward predicting failure, as it signals potential challenges in sustaining strong academic habits through law school. Your first-year grades, also in the lowest decile well below average, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, suggesting an early pattern that worried the model about long-term readiness for the bar. On a more positive note, your LSAT score pulled in the opposite direction, nudging the prediction toward success since it's close to the average of 36.7 seen in students who typically pass. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your low deciles in school performance are a stark outlier, while your other credentials like GPA and family background aligned more closely with averages but couldn't outweigh those red flags. The model prioritizes these early indicators because they've historically been the best predictors of bar outcomes across thousands of students. While this decision feels tough, especially given your full-time commitment and solid LSAT effort, it reflects a data-driven caution rather than a judgment on your potential. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps through community college refreshers or targeted tutoring, and aim to retake the LSAT for a higher score. Building a stronger narrative around academic improvement in your next application, maybe with letters from mentors who can speak to your resilience as a Black man navigating these challenges, could shift the odds. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—your next steps could rewrite this story.
